title: Why Intuition Is Often Dismissed as Irrational
meta_description: Learn why your gut feelings get labeled irrational and how to trust them anyway. Direct truth about intuition.
og_title: Why Intuition Is Often Dismissed as Irrational
og_description: Learn why your gut feelings get labeled irrational and how to trust them anyway. Direct truth about intuition.
body:
Why Intuition Is Often Dismissed as Irrational

You've been there. You know something. Not because you can prove it, not because you have a spreadsheet or a logical argument. You just know. A feeling in your gut. A quiet voice in your head. A sudden knowing that someone's about to call, or that you shouldn't take that job, or that something's wrong with a person everyone else thinks is great.

And then someone says it. Maybe out loud, maybe in their eyes. "That's not rational." "You're being emotional." "Where's your evidence?"

You feel that familiar shame. That old wound. The one that says you're too much, too sensitive, not logical enough. You start to doubt yourself. Maybe they're right. Maybe you are just making things up.

Let me tell you something straight: Nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's. You're picking up on information that the rational mind can't access. And the reason intuition gets dismissed as "irrational" isn't because it's wrong. It's because we live in a culture that worships the left brain and has no vocabulary for the right.

The Problem with "Rational"

Here's what nobody tells you: The rational mind is slow. It's linear. It processes information one piece at a time, like a computer running a single program. And it's amazing for certain things - balancing a checkbook, following a recipe, building a bridge. But it's terrible for the things that actually matter in life. Things like reading a room, sensing danger, knowing who to trust, making a decision when you don't have all the facts.

Intuition works differently. It's fast. It's parallel. It takes in thousands of data points at once - body language, tone of voice, energy, timing, pattern recognition from a lifetime of experience - and it spits out a feeling. A knowing. A nudge. You don't get the step-by-step reasoning. You just get the answer.

And that's exactly why the rational mind hates it. Because the rational mind wants to see the steps. It wants to audit the process. It wants to say "show your work." But intuition doesn't work that way. It's not a math problem. It's a whole-body intelligence that processes information faster than your conscious brain can track.

"The rational mind wants to see the steps. Intuition just hands you the answer. And that's not a flaw. It's a feature. You're not broken because you can't explain how you know. You're operating at a speed the rational mind can't keep up with."

Why Society Dismisses What It Can't Measure

Think about how we're trained from childhood. School rewards the kid who raises their hand and explains their reasoning. It rewards the kid who shows their work. It punishes the kid who says "I just know" - even when that kid is right. Right?!

We're raised in a system that values proof over wisdom. Evidence over experience. Data over discernment. And that system has done a lot of good - don't get me wrong. Science is incredible. Logic is useful. But we've made a terrible mistake: we've decided that what can't be measured doesn't exist.

That's a problem, because most of the important things in life can't be measured. Love. Trust. Timing. Purpose. Danger. Connection. These things aren't quantifiable. You can't put them in a spreadsheet. But you can feel them. And if you've been told your whole life that your feelings are unreliable, you learn to ignore them. You learn to distrust the very thing that's trying to keep you safe.

The Bright Wound

I call this "the bright wound." It's the pain of being sensitive in a world that doesn't value sensitivity. It's the ache of knowing things you can't prove. It's the loneliness of being right when everyone else is wrong, but having no evidence to show for it.

You've felt this. I know you have. You've been in a room where the energy was off, and everyone else acted like everything was fine. You've known a relationship was over months before it ended. You've had a feeling about someone that turned out to be true, but by the time you could prove it, the damage was already done.

That's the bright wound. It's the gift that feels like a curse because nobody taught you how to use it. Nobody validated it. Nobody said "Yes, that's real. That's valuable. Let me help you understand it." Instead, they said "You're overthinking." "You're too sensitive." "You're imagining things."

You're not imagining things. You're just tuned to a frequency most people can't hear.

What Intuition Actually Is (Not What You've Been Told)

Let's clear something up right now. Intuition isn't magic. It's not psychic powers (though that can be part of it for some people). It's not random guessing. It's not wishful thinking. It's not the universe sending you signs.

Intuition is a biological and psychological process. It's your brain making lightning-fast calculations based on patterns you've absorbed over a lifetime. It's your body's autonomic nervous system picking up on cues your conscious mind missed. It's your survival instinct, refined by evolution, operating at full speed.

Malcolm Gladwell wrote about this in his book Blink. He called it "thin-slicing" - the ability to make accurate judgments with very little information. And the research backs it up. People who trust their gut often make better decisions than people who overthink. Not always. But often enough to matter.

If you want to dig deeper into how this works, I'd recommend checking out Sonia Choquette's "Trust Your Vibes" on Amazon (paid link). She breaks down the mechanics of intuition in a way that makes sense for people who've been told their whole lives that they're wrong.

The Real Reason People Dismiss Intuition

Here's the uncomfortable truth: People dismiss intuition because it threatens the status quo. If you can know things without being told, then the systems of power lose their grip. If you can trust your own knowing, then you don't need as many authorities telling you what to do. You don't need as many experts, gurus, or leaders. You become your own authority.

That's scary for people who benefit from your doubt. It's scary for institutions that rely on compliance. It's scary for anyone who's built their identity on being the "rational one" - because if intuition is real, then their entire worldview is incomplete.

Does that land? Think about it. The people who dismiss your intuition the most are often the ones who have the most to lose if you start trusting yourself. It's not about you. It's about their need to stay in control.

How to Tell the Difference Between Intuition and Fear

Now, I'm not saying every feeling you have is intuition. There's a difference between a gut knowing and a fear response. And learning to tell the difference is one of the most important skills you can develop.

Intuition feels clear. It's neutral. It doesn't have a story attached. It's just a knowing. "Don't go left. Go right." No drama. No anxiety. No list of reasons why. Just a simple, clean signal.

Fear, on the other hand, is noisy. It comes with a story. "If I go left, something terrible will happen. Remember that time something bad happened? This is just like that. What if I make the wrong choice? What if everyone judges me?" Fear loops. It repeats. It spirals. It keeps you stuck.

Intuition moves you forward. It's a yes or a no. A toward or an away. It's simple. It's direct. It doesn't need you to justify it. It just asks you to trust it.

If you're struggling with this distinction, I highly recommend Caroline Myss's "Sacred Contracts" on Amazon (paid link). She has a way of cutting through the noise and helping you identify what's actually yours versus what's borrowed from fear or other people's expectations.

Why Your Nervous System Knows Before Your Brain Does

Here's something fascinating: Your body processes information faster than your conscious brain. Your gut has its own nervous system - the enteric nervous system - with over 100 million neurons. It's often called the "second brain" for a reason. It's constantly sampling your environment, picking up on chemical signals, reading the room at a level you can't consciously access.

Have you ever walked into a room and felt the tension before anyone spoke? That's not imagination. That's your nervous system doing its job. It's scanning for threat, for safety, for connection cues. It's doing in milliseconds what your conscious mind would take minutes to figure out.

And here's the kicker: Your body is almost always right. It's been doing this since you were born. It knows what safe feels like. It knows what danger feels like. It knows when someone's lying, when something's off, when an opportunity is actually a trap. The problem isn't your body. The problem is that you've been trained to override it.

Rebuilding Trust in Your Own Knowing

So how do you start trusting yourself again? How do you unlearn all the training that told you your intuition was wrong?

First, you start small. You don't need to make life-altering decisions based on a feeling. Start with the little things. Which way to walk. What to eat. Whether to call that person back today or tomorrow. Pay attention to the feeling, make a choice, and see what happens. Collect evidence that your intuition works.

Second, you stop apologizing for it. Stop saying "I know this sounds crazy but..." Stop prefacing your knowing with disclaimers. Just say "I have a feeling about this" and let that be enough. You don't have to prove it. You don't have to justify it. You're allowed to know things without a PowerPoint presentation.

Third, you surround yourself with people who get it. Find the other sensitive ones. The ones who also know things before they happen. The ones who also feel the energy in a room. The ones who don't make you explain yourself. They're out there. And when you find them, hold on tight. They'll remind you that you're not crazy.

For a deeper dive into this process, The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker on Amazon (paid link) is essential reading. He explains exactly how your intuition works as a survival mechanism and why ignoring it is the most dangerous thing you can do.

The Cost of Ignoring Your Intuition

Let's be real about what happens when you keep dismissing your intuition. You end up in the wrong relationships. You take the wrong jobs. You stay in places that drain you. You say yes when you mean no. You ignore the red flags until they become emergencies. You live a life that looks good on paper but feels hollow inside.

I've seen it a thousand times. The person who knew they shouldn't marry their partner but went ahead anyway because "it was the logical choice." The person who knew they should leave their job but stayed because "the money was good." The person who knew something was wrong with their health but didn't go to the doctor because "I'm probably overreacting."

Your intuition isn't there to make your life harder. It's there to make your life possible. It's your internal guidance system. It's the part of you that knows your path even when you can't see the whole road. And every time you ignore it, you're telling yourself that you don't matter. That your knowing doesn't count. That you're not worth listening to.

That's the bright wound. And it's time to stop bleeding.

What It Looks Like to Trust Yourself

I want you to imagine something. Imagine what it would feel like to trust your intuition completely. Not blindly. Not without discernment. But with the same confidence you have in your rational mind.

Imagine making a decision based on a feeling and not needing to explain it to anyone. Imagine saying "I just know" and letting that be enough. Imagine walking into a room, feeling the energy, and acting on that information without second-guessing yourself.

That's not fantasy. That's available to you. It's available right now. But it requires you to stop asking permission to trust yourself. It requires you to stop outsourcing your knowing to people who don't have access to your inner world.

If you're ready to go deeper into this work, The Psychic Pathway by Sonia Choquette on Amazon (paid link) is a practical guide for people who are ready to stop hiding from their sensitivity and start using it as the gift it actually is.

The Truth About Irrationality

Here's what I want you to walk away with: Intuition isn't irrational. It's pre-rational. It's the information that comes before the rational mind can catch up. It's not the enemy of logic. It's the source that logic draws from.

Every great scientist, artist, entrepreneur, and leader will tell you the same thing. The big breakthroughs didn't come from spreadsheets. They came from a flash of knowing. A sudden insight. A feeling that wouldn't go away. The rational mind then takes that flash and builds a approach around it. But the spark? That's intuition.

So the next time someone tells you that your intuition is irrational, you can smile and say "You're right. It's beyond rational. It's deeper. And that's exactly why it works."

You don't need to defend yourself. You don't need to prove your knowing. You just need to keep listening. Keep trusting. Keep following the thread of your own deep wisdom. Because it knows the way. It's always known the way. The only question is whether you're ready to follow it.

That's the work. That's the path. And you're already on it.

If this article connected with you and you're ready to go deeper into understanding your intuitive nature, take the assessment at intuitivesawaken.com/assessment. It'll help you identify where you are in your journey and what your next step is. No fluff. No BS. Just honest guidance for people who are done pretending they're not sensitive.
